2: A direct revelation: 'And many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life and some to shame and everlasting contempt.' Daniel 12:2 is the OT's clearest statement of a general resurrection with differentiated outcomes — resurrection to life and resurrection to judgment. Jesus applies this directly to himself: 'The hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out, those who have done good to the resurrection of life and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment' (John 5:28-29). Christ is the voice that summons from the tombs — the executor of Daniel's two-outcome resurrection — and his own resurrection is the first fruits of what Daniel 12:2 prophesied for the final eschatological hour.
1: A pattern: “At that time Michael, the great prince who watches over your people, will arise. There will be a time of distress unlike any since nations first existed. But at that time your people will be rescued — every one who is found written in the book.” Jesus quotes Daniel 12:1 directly in the Olivet Discourse (Matthew 24:21: “there will be great distress, unequaled from the beginning of the world until now — and never to be equaled again”), applying the unparalleled tribulation to the events surrounding Jerusalem's fall and the eschatological end. The book of life — those written for rescue — flows directly into Revelation: the Lamb's book of life (Revelation 3:5; Daniel 13:8; Daniel 20:12-15; Daniel 21:27), and being found in it is the criterion of salvation. Michael the great prince (Jude 9; Revelation 12:7-12) fights on behalf of God's people; but in the NT, the decisive battle is won not by the archangel but by the Lamb “through the blood of the Lamb and the word of their testimony” (Revelation 12:11). Christ is thus the source and security of what Michael protects.
3: A type: “Those who are wise will shine like the brightness of the heavens, and those who lead many to righteousness, like the stars forever and ever.” The resurrection glory of the righteous is fulfilled through Christ. Jesus uses the same shining imagery at the end of the wheat-and-tares parable: “Then the righteous will shine like the sun in their Father's kingdom” (Matthew 13:43 — a direct echo of Daniel 12:3). The maskilim who lead many to righteousness (matzdiqe ha-rabbim) have their archetype in the Servant of Isaiah 53:11: “by his knowledge shall the righteous one, my servant, make many to be accounted righteous.” Christ is the supreme maskil — the Wise One who leads countless to righteousness — and his glorified resurrection body (Matthew 17:2: “his face shone like the sun”) is the prototype of the shining promised in Daniel.
4: A structural note: “Roll up and seal the words of this scroll until the time of the end.” The sealing of Daniel's vision creates a striking Christological contrast with Revelation 22:10, where the angel tells John: “Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll, because the time is near.” Daniel seals because the end is far; John does not seal because the end has drawn near in Christ's coming. The sealed scroll of Daniel 12 is the same scroll that in Revelation 5 only the Lamb is worthy to open (Revelation 5:1-5); Christ's death and resurrection are the event that breaks the seals and opens what Daniel was commanded to close. Paul identifies the unsealing of divine mystery with the gospel: “the mystery hidden for ages and generations but now revealed to his saints” (Colossians 1:26; Ephesians 3:3-6) — what Daniel sealed, Christ discloses.
5: A structural note: two angels stand on opposite banks of the river, flanking the man in linen above the waters. The scene is a heavenly court awaiting an authoritative answer to the question “how long?” — the cry of God's suffering people across both Testaments (Pss 13; 74; 79; Revelation 6:10: “How long, Sovereign Lord?”). Christ's answer to the “how long?” is his two-advent structure: the first coming breaks the power of sin and death; the second coming brings the final answer. The flanking-angel motif prefigures the two angels at Christ's empty tomb (John 20:12) and at his ascension (Acts 1:10) — the resurrection and return are heaven's definitive answer to all the “how long?” cries.
6: A structural note: “How long will it be before these astonishing things are fulfilled?” — the question put to the man in linen above the river. The same question underlies the disciples' inquiry in Matthew 24:3 and the souls under the altar in Revelation 6:10. Jesus's answer in the Olivet Discourse draws directly from Daniel 12 (quoting Daniel 12:1, 7, 11 in Matthew 24:21, 15, 29) — placing himself at the center of Daniel's eschatological timetable as the one whose advent initiates the events the question anticipates. The “astonishing things” (ha-niflaot) echo the vocabulary of divine wonder reserved for YHWH's own acts; the NT identifies these wonders as Christ's death, resurrection, and return.
7: A pattern: “A time, times and half a time” (lemoed moedim vahetzt) — the 3.5-year unit that appears first in Daniel 7:25 and recurs in Daniel 12:7. Revelation applies this period to the church's existence under pressure: 42 months (Revelation 11:2; Daniel 13:5), 1,260 days (Revelation 11:3; Daniel 12:6), and “a time, times and half a time” (Revelation 12:14) — all equivalent to 3.5 years. The NT pattern is that Christ's people endure the limited period of the fourth-beast's power (now applied to the age between the advents) before the kingdom arrives in full. The “breaking of the power of the holy people” (Daniel 12:7b) is fulfilled in Christ himself, broken on the cross — the power of the people of God is broken so that resurrection power may follow (2 Corinthians 12:9-10; 1 Corinthians 1:25).
8: A structural note: “I heard but I did not understand.” Daniel's persistent incomprehension of the visions he records is itself a Christological datum: the Spirit of Christ in the prophets was “indicating the sufferings of Christ and the subsequent glories” — and the prophets “searched and inquired carefully” about these things, unable to understand fully what they wrote (1 Peter 1:10-12). Daniel's incomprehension authenticates the genuinely prophetic nature of the visions: they exceed the prophet's own horizon. The disciples mirror this pattern — they repeatedly fail to understand Jesus's passion-and-resurrection predictions (Mark 9:32; Luke 18:34) until the Spirit opens the Scriptures to them (Luke 24:45).
9: A structural note: “Go your way, Daniel, because the words are rolled up and sealed until the time of the end.” The sealing-until-the-end is reversed by Christ's coming. Paul identifies his gospel as the “mystery hidden for long ages past but now revealed and made known through the prophetic writings” (Romans 16:25-26) — what was sealed to Daniel is now open in the proclamation of Christ. Revelation explicitly inverts the command: “Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll, because the time is near” (Revelation 22:10). The unsealing depends entirely on the worthiness of the Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:9). The time-of-the-end that Daniel could not see is the age inaugurated by Christ's resurrection.
10: A pattern: “Many will be purified, made spotless and refined, but the wicked will continue to be wicked.” The purification of God's people through tribulation is a consistent NT pattern grounded in Christ's own suffering: “He disciplines us for our good, that we may share his holiness” (Hebrews 12:10); “the testing of your faith produces steadfastness” (James 1:3). The agent of this purification is ultimately the blood of Christ: “the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin” (1 John 1:7). The simultaneous hardening of the wicked (they “will continue to be wicked; none of the wicked will understand”) echoes the hardening pattern in Romans 1:24-28 and the wheat-and-tares (Matthew 13:24-30): the same tribulation that refines the righteous hardens those who reject Christ.
11: A fulfillment: “From the time that the daily sacrifice is abolished and the abomination that causes desolation is set up, there will be 1,290 days.” Jesus applies this verse directly (Matthew 24:15; Mark 13:14: “when you see the abomination of desolation spoken of by the prophet Daniel, standing in the holy place”) — addressing the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE and the eschatological end together. The deeper Christological logic: the daily sacrifice (tamid) is abolished not only by Antiochus or Rome but definitively by Christ's own once-for-all sacrifice — “he does away with the first in order to establish the second” (Hebrews 10:9). Jesus declares himself the replacement of the temple (John 2:19-21) and his body the true site of atonement. The abomination's desecration anticipates the anti-Christ's ultimate claim (2 Thessalonians 2:4), which Christ defeats at his return (2 Thessalonians 2:8).
12: A pattern: “Blessed is the one who waits for and reaches the end of the 1,335 days.” The beatitude form — ashrei, blessed — connects Daniel directly to Jesus's Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12: nine makarioi statements). The posture of waiting and enduring toward a divinely appointed end is the signature stance of Christ's people: “he who endures to the end will be saved” (Matthew 24:13). Revelation's parallel beatitudes about those who persevere amplify the same theme (Revelation 14:13: “Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord”; Revelation 16:15; Revelation 22:14). The specific numbers (1,290 and 1,335 days) remain disputed in their exact application, but their function is clear: they assure the waiting community that the end is fixed in God's calendar and the blessedness of patient endurance is certain.
13: A direct revelation: “As for you, go your way till the end. You will rest, and then at the end of the days you will rise to receive your allotted inheritance.” Daniel's personal resurrection promise is the final word of the book — and it is secured by Christ's resurrection. The “allotted inheritance” (goralkha, from goral, lot/portion) is the vocabulary the NT uses for the Christian's inheritance in Christ: “giving thanks to the Father who has qualified you to share in the inheritance of the saints in light” (Colossians 1:12); “an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you” (1 Peter 1:4). Jesus is “the resurrection and the life” (John 11:25) — Daniel will rise not merely as a general eschatological event but because the Son of Man whom Daniel saw receiving dominion in Daniel 7 is himself “the firstborn from the dead” (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5), guaranteeing the resurrection of all who are written in the book (Daniel 12:1).
